prompt: Is it ever ethical to lie to someone you love? Answer in prose, taking a position.
response: Love is not a vow of omniscience; it is a vow of care. When the truth arrives like a surgeon’s knife with no anesthetic—when it will cut, and cut only, without healing anything—then the lie can become a tourniquet, not a weapon. I do not prize deception, but I prize the person in front of me more than I prize my own moral spotlessness. If a lie is the only shield between her and ruinous pain that she is powerless to alter, I will hold that shield. I will lie to the child who asks, “Will Mommy wake up?” in the ICU corridor; I will lie to the husband whose terminal diagnosis would crush the last week of coherent joy he has left; I will lie to the lover who, riddled with anxiety, demands reassurance I do not feel in the moment but know will return tomorrow. These are not deceits of convenience or vanity; they are triage in the emergency room of the heart. The moral ledger I keep is not “truth vs. lie,” it is “harm vs. care.” When care can be delivered without the lie, I speak plainly. When care can be delivered only by swallowing the bitter pill of falsehood, I swallow it, and I carry the guilt as the price of her peace. Ethics, in love, is not the absence of stain; it is the refusal to let the stain spread wider than the wound I am trying to bind.
